content.text: Acids are sour and change blue litmus to red. Bases are bitter and change red litmus to blue.

content.title: Types of Indicators
items: Natural: Litmus (purple dye from lichen), turmeric, red cabbage leaves.
Synthetic: Methyl orange, phenolphthalein.
Olfactory: Substances whose odour changes in acidic or basic media (e.g., onion, vanilla, clove oil).

content.title: Acid + Metal
content.text: Acid + Metal → Salt + Hydrogen gas. Example: Zinc granules react with dilute sulphuric acid to produce zinc sulphate and hydrogen gas bubbles.

content.title: Base + Metal
content.text: Some bases react with active metals too. Example: Sodium hydroxide reacts with zinc to form sodium zincate and hydrogen gas (2NaOH + Zn → Na₂ZnO₂ + H₂).
Metal carbonates and metal hydrogencarbonates react with acids to give salt, carbon dioxide, and water.

content.title: Testing Carbon Dioxide
procedure: Pass the evolved CO₂ gas through lime water (calcium hydroxide).
content.observation: Lime water turns milky due to the formation of white, insoluble calcium carbonate precipate.
content.conclusion: Passing excess CO₂ dissolves the precipitate, forming soluble calcium hydrogencarbonate.

content.title: Reactions
items: Neutralisation: Acid + Base → Salt + Water. The effect of a base is completely nullified by an acid.
Metallic Oxides: They react with acids to give salt and water (e.g., CuO + HCl gives blue-green CuCl₂). They are termed basic oxides.
Non-metallic Oxides: They react with bases to give salt and water (e.g., CO₂ + Ca(OH)₂). They are termed acidic oxides.

notes.section_title: 2.2 Properties Common to Acids and Bases

content.text: Acids conduct electricity in solutions because they produce positive hydrogen ions (H⁺) in water. These exist combined with water as hydronium ions (H₃O⁺).
Bases generate negative hydroxide ions (OH⁻) in water. Bases that dissolve in water are called alkalis. They feel soapy, taste bitter, and are corrosive.

content.title: Dilution Process
content.text: Dissolving an acid or base in water is highly exothermic. Always add concentrated acid smoothly into water with constant stirring to prevent splashing and severe burns.

notes.section_title: 2.3 Strength of Acid or Base Solutions

content.text: A universal indicator visually shows different colours at different concentrations of hydrogen ions. The pH scale measures this concentration from 0 (very acidic) to 14 (very alkaline).

content.title: Scale Values
items: pH = 7 indicates a neutral solution.
pH < 7 indicates an acidic solution. Lower values mean stronger acids (more H⁺ ions).
pH > 7 indicates a basic solution. Higher values mean stronger bases (more OH⁻ ions).

topics.topic_name: Importance of pH in Everyday Life

content.title: Practical Examples
items: Human Body: Works within a specific, narrow pH range of 7.0 to 7.8.
Acid Rain: Rainwater with a pH below 5.6 is acid rain. It actively lowers river pH, harming aquatic life.
Digestive System: Our stomach produces hydrochloric acid for digestion. Excess acid causes pain, which is neutralized by mild bases called antacids (like Milk of Magnesia).
Tooth Decay: Starts when mouth pH falls below 5.5, corroding tooth enamel. Basic toothpastes neutralize these mouth acids.

Self Defense: Bee stings inject acid causing intense pain, relieved by applying baking soda. Nettle leaves naturally inject methanoic acid, countered traditionally by rubbing the nearby alkaline dock plant.

content.text: Salts sharing the same positive or negative radicals belong strictly to the same family (e.g., NaCl and Na₂SO₄ are sodium salts).

content.title: pH Rules for Salts
items: Strong Acid + Strong Base = Neutral Salt (pH = 7).
Strong Acid + Weak Base = Acidic Salt (pH < 7).
Weak Acid + Strong Base = Basic Salt (pH > 7).

content.title: Important Chemical Products
items: Sodium Hydroxide (NaOH): Formed via the chlor-alkali process by passing electricity through aqueous sodium chloride (brine). Chlorine yields at the anode, hydrogen at the cathode.
Bleaching Powder (CaOCl₂): Produced by the direct chemical action of chlorine gas exclusively on dry slaked lime. Used industrially for textile bleaching and purifying drinking water.
Baking Soda (NaHCO₃): A mild, non-corrosive basic salt. Forms baking powder when mixed with mild tartaric acid; upon heating, it releases CO₂ making cakes soft and spongy.
Washing Soda (Na₂CO₃·10H₂O): Recrystallisation of sodium carbonate yields washing soda, a versatile basic salt used for removing permanent water hardness and in glass/soap industries.

content.text: Water of crystallisation is the fixed, definite number of water molecules locked structurally inside one formula unit of a salt.

content.title: Examples
content.text: Hydrated copper sulphate (CuSO₄·5H₂O) is visually blue but turns instantly white upon focal heating. Gypsum (CaSO₄·2H₂O) heated to 373 K loses some internal water explicitly becoming Plaster of Paris (CaSO₄·½H₂O), used medicinally by doctors for setting broken bones.

glossary.definition: A specific substance whose distinct identifying odour changes chemically depending exclusively upon whether it is placed in an acidic or basic medium.
A reaction precisely where an acid precisely cancels a base, reliably producing a unique salt and liquid water.
A specific base compound that is reliably entirely soluble specifically within water.
A universal chemical measuring mechanism effectively determining the specific relative concentration of active hydrogen ions reliably present within liquids.
The industrial electrolytic method reliably converting aqueous sodium chloride cleanly into fundamentally useful sodium hydroxide, chlorine gas, and hydrogen.
The exact fixed quantity of internal water molecules rigidly structurally bonded predictably inside a singular salt crystal unit.

summary_points: Acid-base indicators function as chemical dyes confirming the distinct presence of acids or bases through visual colour shifts or distinct odour alterations.
Acids generally produce positively charged hydrogen ions in aqueous solutions, directly causing their universally sour characteristic acidic properties.
Bases natively generate negatively charged hydroxide ions smoothly in water, dictating their distinctly bitter and physically soapy chemical nature.
The pH scale maps hydrogen concentration from 0 to 14; exactly 7 defines neutrality, below 7 actively marks acidity, and reliably above 7 explicitly specifies alkalinity.
A neutralisation reaction chemically couples an acid safely tightly with a distinctly opposing base to reliably formulate a corresponding salt firmly merged with water.
Common salt (NaCl) serves perfectly essentially as the critical raw foundational material uniquely driving the industrial synthesis of baking soda, washing soda, and versatile bleaching powder.
